reasoning: The FRED statistical tool provided a primary anchor of 0.70 for a short-term increase, largely driven by the linear regression pulling the prediction towards the recent historical mean of ~3.63 after a drop to 3.57. I moderated this slightly to 0.65 for the 1-week horizon (2026-04-19) to account for the clear short-term downward momentum (3 straight declines). Over the 1-to-12 month horizons, the Fed is likely continuing its rate-cutting cycle toward a neutral rate (estimated around 2.75% to 3.0%), making an increase from the 3.57 baseline progressively less likely, dropping probabilities to ~0.35 for late 2026 and mid-2027. For the extended 3-year, 5-year, and 10-year horizons, macroeconomic regimes become highly unpredictable, so probabilities steadily regress back to 0.50 as uncertainty dominates.
